title: Managed Agents in the Gemini API
summary: Google's Managed Agents service provides one-API-call autonomous agents that run in remote Linux sandboxes, powered by Gemini 3.5 Flash and the Antigravity Agent Harness. The system includes an Interactions API with agent-native documentation and skills, demonstrated turning Hacker News threads into radio shows using Gemini TTS and Lyria.
writeup: Two members of the Gemini API team walk through the launch of managed agents in the Gemini API: a single API call that provisions an isolated Linux sandbox where a model can run code, execute bash commands, invoke sub-agents, and call tools like Gemini TTS and Lyria without touching a developer's production servers. The demo shows a radio-production app built on top of managed agents that ingests 18 Hacker News sources, writes a script with opposing viewpoints, generates voices via Gemini TTS, and produces a thumbnail, all from one API call. The through-line is the shift from a turn-based generate-content model to an agent-first data model where tools, skills, and environment steps are first-class citizens of the API alongside the user and model roles.
key_points: A managed agent in the Gemini API runs inside a remote, isolated Linux sandbox, so the agent can write and execute code, run bash commands, and create files without risk to the caller's production infrastructure.

The Interactions API replaces the older generate-content API as the unified interface for both models and agents, with a data model that natively represents tool calls, sub-agent invocations, and environment steps as distinct interaction types rather than forcing everything into a user/model turn structure.
Deep Research was the first managed agent shipped under the Interactions API, establishing the pattern before the general managed-agent capability was opened to developers.

Managed agents are defined using plain markdown files: an agents.md file for system instructions and one markdown file per skill, making agent definitions readable by both humans and coding agents and writable without a custom DSL.

Google rebuilt its API documentation to be agent-first, exposing all docs as accessible markdown with an MCP server so a coding agent in an IDE can search the docs and iteratively build and deploy a managed agent in the same session.

The demo radio-production app combines managed agents with Gemini TTS, the Lyria music model, and a thumbnail generator across 18 sources, producing a coherent three-minute radio show from a single API call.
